text: The surgeon begins the operation by developing a peritoneal flap, a standard initial step in robotic inguinal hernia repair.
The video shows the prominent arcuate line in the patient's abdominal wall, along with a thin peritoneum and transversalis fascia visible during the robotic inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon begins the standard dissection of the direct hernia, using minimally invasive surgical techniques to carefully separate the hernia sac from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins lateral dissection, focusing on separating tissue layers in the lateral aspect of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the peritoneum and transversalis fascia, which appear adhered to each other. The dissection focuses on entering the correct plane within the transversalis fascia to access the true preperitoneal space.
The surgeon begins developing a peritoneal flap, advancing towards the hernia site, with visible manipulation of the peritoneal tissue.
The surgeon performs the reduction of the small direct hernia, manipulating the herniated tissue back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon continues the dissection to develop the appropriate preperitoneal space for mesh placement, ensuring separation of the peritoneum from the transversalis fascia.
The surgeon performs retro pubic dissection, focusing on identifying the pubis, Cooper's ligament, and the operator space.
The surgeon begins the initial parietalization of anatomical structures, followed by returning to work in the midline area.
The surgeon performs parietalization of the structures, holding a continuation of the medial ligament visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the midline area, addressing the direct hernia by ensuring thorough dissection and exposure of the relevant anatomical structures.
The surgeon exposes the external iliac artery and proceeds to parietalize the vas deferens, which can be visually challenging due to its anatomical position.
The surgeon uses tension and counter-tension techniques to grasp and manipulate anatomical structures, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon checks for a cord lipoma and identifies the iliopubic tract, pointing it out for visual confirmation.
The surgeon visually confirms the absence of a cord lipoma upon identifying the iliopubic tract.
The surgeon broadens the dissection space to achieve a clear view of the myopectineal orifice, ensuring a definitive critical view of the anatomical area.
A small portion of the vein is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the pubic tubercle across the midline, rules out any direct hernias, and dissects two centimeters between Cooper's ligament and the bladder. The dissection continues between Cooper's ligament and the iliac vein to identify any femoral defects. The indirect sac and peritoneum are dissected if present, and any cord lipomas are identified or reduced. The peritoneum is dissected lateral to the cord, preparing for adequate mesh placement.
The surgeon places a mid-weight mesh over a small direct defect, securing it at multiple points along Cooper's ligament on both the ipsilateral and contralateral sides.
The surgeon double secures the mesh underneath the defect to Cooper's ligament, avoiding a pulsating vessel nearby. The mesh is then secured to the contralateral Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon begins the closure of the peritoneal flap after fixating the mesh, ensuring there are no peritoneal holes visible from the dissection. The suture is run continuously to close the flap.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum, securing it to the transversal fascia and the posterior rectus sheath, creating a sturdy closure. The prominent articulate line is visible during this process.
